title: Sectors
excerpt: A Finnish tech company specialising in automated 3D printing and the end-to-end production of low-carbon concrete foundations for infrastructure has announced $7.4 m in growth funding.
article_text:
The round into Espoo-based Hyperion Robotics was co-led by Course Corrected and the European Innovation Council Fund (EIC Fund), with participation from RE Ventures and existing investors Lifeline Ventures, Übermorgen Ventures and PC Rettig Impact & Co.

Hyperion said it will use the funding to scale its robotic microfactories across Europe.

Compared to traditional methods, Hyperion reports that its robotic microfactories produce infrastructure components up to three times faster, cut costs by up to 50 per cent and reduce CO₂ emissions by up to 70 per cent. The company is replacing labour-intensive construction with a factory-based model, deployed close to project sites to reduce logistics, speed up delivery and enable consistent, localised and more cost-efficient production for European infrastructure.

MORE FROM POLICY & BUSINESS

Hyperion's approach is built on physical AI; at its core is Forge, a software platform that connects design, structural engineering, code-compliance, robotics and factory operations into a single system.

Hyperion's first UK site, Forge I, will launch in Flixborough near Scunthorpe in partnership with LKAB . The facility will produce infrastructure components for sectors including energy, utilities, water, data centres and carbon capture.

In a statement, Fernando De los Rios, CEO of Hyperion Robotics, said: “We've already built some of the most efficient concrete structures in the world. With this funding, we start delivering at scale, in factories built next to the projects they serve. Europe doesn't have the time, the budget or the labour for construction to keep working the way it has. Physical AI is how we close that gap.”

The growth funding brings Hyperion's total capital raised to nearly $20m as the company moves from project-by-project delivery to industrial-scale production. Hyperion already counts National Grid, Costain, Mott MacDonald Bentley, Anglian Water and United Utilities among its clients.
